question: What is the worst-case time complexity of both Bubble Sort and Selection Sort?
explanation: Both Selection Sort and Bubble Sort are simple sorting algorithms that use nested loops to compare and swap elements, resulting in a worst-case and average-case time complexity of O(n²).

question: Which of the following statements best describes a key difference between Bubble Sort and Selection Sort?
options: Bubble Sort is stable while Selection Sort is not.
Selection Sort is stable while Bubble Sort is not.
Bubble Sort requires fewer swaps on average.
Selection Sort has a better worst-case time complexity.
explanation: A standard implementation of Bubble Sort is stable because it only swaps adjacent elements when they are out of order. Selection Sort is generally not stable as it may swap a current element with a non-adjacent element, which can change the relative order of equal keys.

question: After the first complete pass of a Selection Sort algorithm on the array [5, 3, 8, 1], what will the array look like?
explanation: Selection Sort finds the smallest element (1) in the first pass and swaps it with the element at the first index (5). The array becomes [1, 3, 8, 5] after the first pass.

question: In an optimized Bubble Sort implementation, what does a pass with no swaps indicate?
options: The array is now sorted and the algorithm can terminate early.
The smallest element has been placed at the end.
The algorithm has encountered its worst-case scenario.
explanation: An optimized Bubble Sort can track if any swaps were made during a pass. If no swaps occur, it means the array is already in order, and the algorithm can break out of the loop early, improving its best-case time complexity to O(n).

question: Considering the number of comparisons and swaps, which statement is TRUE for the worst-case scenario of these algorithms?
options: Selection Sort always makes more comparisons than Bubble Sort.
Bubble Sort always makes more swaps than Selection Sort.
Selection Sort makes O(n) swaps while Bubble Sort makes O(n²) swaps.
Both algorithms make the same number of comparisons and swaps.
explanation: Selection Sort makes O(n) swaps because it only performs one swap per element after finding the minimum. In the worst case, Bubble Sort performs a swap on nearly every comparison, leading to O(n²) swaps. Both algorithms make O(n²) comparisons.
